answer:
**Allama Iqbal's poem "Bilad-e-Islamiya" ("The Abodes of Islam") from *Bang-E-Dara Part 3* celebrates key cities of the Islamic world—Delhi, Baghdad, Cordoba, Constantinople, and Yathrib (Medina)—as sacred sites imbued with historical, spiritual, and civilizational significance.**[^1]

The poem opens with Delhi:

> The soil of Delhi is a shrine for the grief-stricken heart;  
> In every particle, the blood of our forebears lies asleep.[^2]  
>   
> How could the earth of this ruined garden not be sacred ground?  
> This land is the sanctum of the majesty of Islam.[^3]

It then evokes Baghdad:

> Though Delhi is a shrine for every Muslim soul,  
> Baghdad, too, is worthy of this sacred honor.  
> This is the garden that became the pride and glory  
> Of that desert tulip known as the culture of Hijaz.[^4]

Cordoba follows:

> The land of Cordoba, too, is the light of a Muslim's eye,  
> A Lamp of Sinai that shone in the darkness of the West.  
> Its flame went out, and scattered the Shining Nation's assembly,  
> Yet in its dying breath, it lit the lamp of the civilization of today.[^5]

Constantinople (Istanbul) is hailed as:

> The realm of Constantinople, once the abode of Caesar,  
> Now an eternal symbol of the Ummah's power and grandeur.  
> Like the dust of the Holy Sanctuary, this land is also pure;  
> It is the threshold of a throne that serves the Lord of Creation's Purpose.[^6]  
>   
> Its very air is as pure as the fragrance of a rose;  
> From the tomb of Ayyub Ansari, a voice arises.  
> "O Muslim! This city is the very heart of the Islamic nation;  
> It is the harvest of centuries of struggle and sacrifice."[^7]

The poem culminates with Medina (Yathrib):

> But you are that sacred land, O resting place of the Chosen One,  
> For the Kaaba itself, a glimpse of you is more than the Great Pilgrimage could ever be.[^8]  
>   
> ...  
>   
> Ah, Yathrib! You are the Muslim's true homeland, you are his sanctuary;  
> You are the focal point that gathers all the rays of our soul's devotion.[^9]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, [Bilad-e-Islamiya](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/).

[^2]: Ibid., [p-876603](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-876603).

[^3]: Ibid., [p-4ecfd3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-4ecfd3).

[^4]: Ibid., [p-c1f59e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c1f59e) to [p-c959b9](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-c959b9).

[^5]: Ibid., [p-8c21df](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-8c21df) to [p-2686fa](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-2686fa).

[^6]: Ibid., [p-e0bfb3](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-e0bfb3) to [p-29900d](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-29900d).

[^7]: Ibid., [p-f14b70](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-f14b70) to [p-630e3b](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-630e3b).

[^8]: Ibid., [p-2a10e2](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-2a10e2).

[^9]: Ibid., [p-f92993](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/02-bilad-e-islamiya/translation/#p-f92993).
